text:
X
r  Distributed systems often comprise numerous autonomous
a computers collaborating to share data,  hardware,  or other
resources [1]. A key principle in these systems  is known
as mutual exclusion, ensuring that only one participant at a
time engages in a critical section where shared resources are
manipulated. Enforcing this principle across multiple machines
without a uniﬁed clock or common memory is the essence of
the Distributed Mutual Exclusion (DME) problem.
In general, the literature distinguishes DME solutions into
two primary categories:
1) Permission-based (non-token) algorithms, such as the
Ricart–Agrawala scheme [2], where a node must acquire
authorization from others  (or  a designated quorum)
before using the critical resource.
2) Token-based  algorithms,  exempliﬁed  by  Suzuki–
Kasami [3], where a speciﬁc token grants unique access
rights to the critical section.

d Distributed Mutual
lgorithms
Sattar Lotﬁ Fatemi †
arif University of Technology, Tehran, Iran
2017@gmail.com
arif University of Technology, Tehran, Iran
e@sharif.edu
We concentrate on token-based methods. Section II cov-
ers the fundamental requirements for mutual exclusion, and
Section III outlines representative token-based protocols that
employ various topological assumptions.  In Section IV, we
compare these algorithms regarding performance indices (e.g.,
message complexity) and fault resilience, plus examine how
they differ from permission-based approaches. Advanced top-
ics such as group-based resource sharing and self-stabilization
are addressed in Section V. Section VII  identiﬁes current
challenges,  including the rising need for robust solutions in
highly mobile networks, opportunities  to leverage machine
learning, and employing blockchain-inspired security. Mean-
while, Section VI  illustrates how an alternate construction
based on ﬁnite projective planes can attain O(1) best-case
complexity and O(√N) in the worst case. The ﬁnal discussion
in Section VIII reﬂects on the overall state of  token-based
DME and where it may develop in coming years.

A. Basic Requirements
Any valid DME solution enforces:
• Safety: Strictly one process occupies the critical section
at a given moment.
• Liveness: Each process that requests the critical section is
eventually allowed to proceed (i.e., no inﬁnite blocking).
• Fairness: Typically,  the order  in which requests are
served follows a queue-based or ﬁrst-come-ﬁrst-served
manner, though some protocols relax fairness to minimize
overhead.
B. Token-based vs. Permission-based Approaches
Permission-based solutions, including Ricart–Agrawala [2],
require that processes explicitly receive responses from other
nodes before entering the critical section. In contrast, token-
based models permit exclusive access to whichever participant
holds a logically unique token, often cutting down on the com-
munication required once a token is in transit. Nevertheless,
designers must guard against situations where the token is
mistakenly duplicated or dropped.
C. Performance Metrics
Key measures include:

Token-based methods commonly incorporate assumptions
about the network layout, ranging from fully connected nodes
to ring or tree formations, mesh conﬁgurations, and more ﬂuid
ad hoc environments [5].
A. Fully Connected Networks
1) Suzuki–Kasami Algorithm [3]: A process broadcasts a
REQUEST whenever  it wants  to enter  the critical  section.
The current  token holder forwards the token upon noticing
an updated request  index. Though O(N) messages may be
used per request, this can still be an improvement over naive
permission-based setups needing 2(N  1) messages.
−
2) Singhal’s Heuristic [9]: Instead of broadcasting widely,
each node identiﬁes a narrower subset of likely token holders,
lowering the number of messages. This is especially beneﬁcial
with frequent critical-section access.
3) Fault-Tolerant Variants [10],  [11]: Some designs ac-
count for lost or crashed token owners, regenerating tokens
with incrementing epoch numbers and invalidating any resid-
ual tokens from older epochs.
B. Tree-Based Algorithms
Token movement can be contained within a spanning tree
that covers all nodes.
1) Raymond’s Tree Algorithm [12]: Messages propagate
along the tree toward the node believed to hold the token.
If the tree remains balanced, each request can be served in
around O(logN) messages.
2) Naimi–Trehel Path Reversal [5]: Pointers are altered as
requests travel, effectively bringing the token closer to nodes
that  request  it most  frequently, which can be advantageous
under skewed demand.

3) Failure Handling:  If a crucial link or node in the tree
fails, local routes must be mended or restructured so that token
access remains feasible.
C. Ring-Based Protocols
1) Simple Token Ring: Here, nodes form a cycle, and the
token goes around in a loop. Under steady demand, each node
obtains the token in a ﬁxed order, but worst-case waiting can
be O(N) if a node just misses the token.
2) Chordal/Shortcut Rings [5]: By adding shortcut links,
token movement speeds up at the expense of introducing extra
rules to ensure the token remains unique and loop-free.
D. Mesh or 2D Grid Topologies
In a two-dimensional grid, nodes typically connect to ad-
jacent neighbors (e.g., up, down, left, right). Strategies might
split request routing along one axis and token movement along
another, yielding complexities between O(√N) and O(N)
depending on how effectively trafﬁc is managed.
E. Dynamic and Ad Hoc Networks
Ad hoc environments such as MANETs or FANETs can see
frequent topological reconﬁgurations [6]–[8].
1) Walter et al. [8]: Maintains a Directed Acyclic Graph
(DAG), adjusting edges as nodes move to avoid cycles and
ensure a singular path to the token holder.
2) FANET Approaches (MRME, RCLME) [6], [7]:
• MRME: Retains a standby token copy to quickly recreate
it if the primary holder vanishes due to mobility issues.
• RCLME: Deploys fuzzy logic to elect  local  leaders in
UAV swarms,  thereby restricting token transmissions to
smaller clusters.
IV. PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS, FAULT TOLERANCE, AND
COMPARISON
A. Message Complexity and Synchronization Delay
• Fully Connected: Algorithms like Suzuki–Kasami entail
about O(N) messages per request, typically superior to
simpler permission-based approaches.
• Tree-Based: Techniques such as Raymond’s can maintain
about O(logN) on balanced trees but degrade if the tree
is skewed.
• Ring-Based: Straightforward, but a process can still wait
O(N) cycles for the token.
• Mesh/2D: Usually between O(√N) and O(N), shaped
by how requests and token routes are organized.
• Dynamic/Ad Hoc: Overheads are highly variable since
nodes may come and go. Clever local caching or adaptiv-
ity mitigates cost but cannot always avoid adverse cases.
B. Fault-Tolerance Mechanisms
1) Node Crash: A timeout-based strategy identiﬁes miss-
ing token holders and regenerates a valid token with a
new version number.
2) Link Failure: In tree or ring topologies, reconﬁgurations
or link bypassing are required; fully connected graphs
can more freely choose alternate links.

A. Message Complexity and Synchronization Delay
• Fully Connected: Algorithms like Suzuki–Kasami entail
about O(N) messages per request, typically superior to
simpler permission-based approaches.
• Tree-Based: Techniques such as Raymond’s can maintain
about O(logN) on balanced trees but degrade if the tree

• Dynamic/Ad Hoc: Overheads are highly variable since

ity mitigates cost but cannot always avoid adverse cases.
B. Fault-Tolerance Mechanisms
1) Node Crash: A timeout-based strategy identiﬁes miss-

new version number.
2) Link Failure: In tree or ring topologies, reconﬁgurations
or link bypassing are required; fully connected graphs
can more freely choose alternate links.

metadata.section_title: Advanced Extensions: Group/k-Mutual Exclusion and Self-Stabilization

text:
A. Group/k-Mutual Exclusion
When multiple processes (up to k) can safely use a resource
simultaneously, k tokens or partial tokens must be circulated.
1) Multi-Token Raymond: A direct extension of Raymond’s
approach that handles multiple tokens concurrently, carefully
preventing conﬂicts when multiple processes request  the re-
source.
2) Other Approaches:  Ring-  and fully connected-based
algorithms can likewise be augmented by tracking how many
tokens are in use and stopping new ones from being issued
once k tokens have been distributed.
B. Self-Stabilizing DME
Self-stabilization [13] indicates the algorithm will return to
a valid single-token state from any erroneous conﬁguration.
1) Kiniwa’s BFS Tree [14]: Nodes create a BFS tree
aimed at the (suspected) token holder. If multiple tokens arise,
collisions resolve so that only one legitimate token remains.
2) Chen & Welch for MANETs [15]: Uses  two distinct
tokens  to handle group membership and mutual exclusion.
Older tokens become invalid once they meet a newer epoch
version.
VI. AN ADDITIONAL TOKEN-BASED METHOD USING
FINITE PROJECTIVE PLANES
Aside from typical  layouts (trees,  rings,  etc.),  a unique
approach employs ﬁnite projective planes to obtain an O(√N)
worst-case overhead and an O(1) best case.
A. High-Level Idea
Nodes are organized into superior and inferior sets through
a projective plane arrangement, providing:
• A single node in common for every pair of arbitrator sets,
removing repeated or overly broad requests.
• Direct knowledge by each node’s superior set on who
holds the token.
• A single physical token, to ensure that local checks con-
ﬁrm no inferior node currently has it before requesting.

B. Algorithm Outline
1)  Initialization: One node starts with the token, notifying
its superior group.
2) Requesting the Token: The requester checks its inferior
set for token possession,  then asks its superiors if the
token is elsewhere.
3) Token Passing: A node holding the token grants it  if
requested or propagates the request to whichever node
is indicated by the projective-plane structure.
4) Exiting the CS: On leaving the critical  section,  the
node updates superiors and, if another request is waiting,
hands over the token.
C. Correctness and Performance
• Mutual Exclusion: A single valid token is needed to
enter the critical section, preventing concurrency issues.
• Starvation Freedom: Requests eventually reach the to-
ken, thanks to a structured approach ensuring at most one
intersection node for each pair of sets.
• Deadlock Avoidance: There is always a path from any
requester to the actual token holder.
• Message Complexity: In the best scenario, no network-
level  searching is needed (O(1)).  In  the worst  case,
requesting nodes may have to consult about O(√N) sets.
This method can outperform some consensus-based strate-
gies (like Maekawa’s)  in scenarios where repeated requests
come from speciﬁc nodes.
VII. OPEN CHALLENGES AND RESEARCH DIRECTIONS
A. Highly Dynamic Networks (FANET, VANET, MANET)
Frequent disconnections and reconnections can lead to lost
or duplicate tokens:
• Partition-Aware Methods: Retain distinct  tokens  for
separate partitions; safely unify these tokens upon merg-
ing networks.
• Energy Minimization: In UAV or sensor setups, com-
munication costs must be carefully limited.
B. Machine Learning Integration
Predictive techniques can streamline token handling:
• Request Forecasting: Position the token closer to nodes
that are likely to require the critical section soon.
• Adaptive Timeout: Use  learning algorithms  to  tune
timeouts, lowering the chance of prematurely concluding
the token is lost.
C. Blockchain-Level Security
If nodes are malicious, they might forge or misuse tokens:
•  Immutable Transfer Logs: Employ ledger-style records
for each token handoff so that inconsistencies stand out.
• Lightweight  Consensus:  Byzantine  fault-tolerant  or
proof-of-authority mechanisms can defend token legiti-
macy in partially trusted networks.

metadata.section_title: An Additional Token-Based Method Using Finite Projective Planes

text:

approach employs ﬁnite projective planes to obtain an O(√N)
worst-case overhead and an O(1) best case.
A. High-Level Idea

• A single node in common for every pair of arbitrator sets,

holds the token.
• A single physical token, to ensure that local checks con-
ﬁrm no inferior node currently has it before requesting.

B. Algorithm Outline

its superior group.
2) Requesting the Token: The requester checks its inferior
set for token possession,  then asks its superiors if the
token is elsewhere.
3) Token Passing: A node holding the token grants it  if
requested or propagates the request to whichever node
is indicated by the projective-plane structure.
4) Exiting the CS: On leaving the critical  section,  the
node updates superiors and, if another request is waiting,

• Mutual Exclusion: A single valid token is needed to

ken, thanks to a structured approach ensuring at most one

• Deadlock Avoidance: There is always a path from any

level  searching is needed (O(1)).  In  the worst  case,
requesting nodes may have to consult about O(√N) sets.
This method can outperform some consensus-based strate-
gies (like Maekawa’s)  in scenarios where repeated requests
come from speciﬁc nodes.

A. Highly Dynamic Networks (FANET, VANET, MANET)
Frequent disconnections and reconnections can lead to lost
or duplicate tokens:
• Partition-Aware Methods: Retain distinct  tokens  for
separate partitions; safely unify these tokens upon merg-
ing networks.
• Energy Minimization: In UAV or sensor setups, com-
munication costs must be carefully limited.
B. Machine Learning Integration
Predictive techniques can streamline token handling:
• Request Forecasting: Position the token closer to nodes
that are likely to require the critical section soon.
• Adaptive Timeout: Use  learning algorithms  to  tune
timeouts, lowering the chance of prematurely concluding
the token is lost.
C. Blockchain-Level Security
If nodes are malicious, they might forge or misuse tokens:
•  Immutable Transfer Logs: Employ ledger-style records
for each token handoff so that inconsistencies stand out.
• Lightweight  Consensus:  Byzantine  fault-tolerant  or
proof-of-authority mechanisms can defend token legiti-
macy in partially trusted networks.

In many distributed scenarios, token-based mutual exclu-
sion remains an effective tactic for governing access to shared
resources. Compared to permission-based strategies that hinge
on multi-party acknowledgments, passing a single authoritative
token can be more communication-efﬁcient  if  the token is
sensibly routed.
•  Inﬂuence of Network Topology: Fully connected setups
lend themselves to simple broadcast-then-token-forward
methods (e.g., Suzuki–Kasami), while tree-based or ring-
based structures offer different  trade-offs  in terms of
messaging or wait times.
• Finite  Projective  Planes  Approach:  By  structur-
ing nodes  into superior/inferior sets, one can achieve
O(1)  best-case messaging  and O(√N)  in  worst
cases—especially helpful if a handful of processes fre-
quently request the resource.
• Mobility and Fault Tolerance: Techniques must handle
token  loss,  duplication,  or node/link failures without
breaking mutual exclusion.
• Promising Enhancements: Group/k-mutual exclusion,
self-stabilization, machine  learning–driven  token  for-
warding, and blockchain-level defenses show how token-
based schemes adapt to modern computational demands,
including UAV ﬂeets and huge data centers.
Looking ahead, token-based DME algorithms offer a robust,
evolving solution path. Whether augmented by specialized
data structures, predictive  intelligence, or  secure handover

records, token passing will likely continue to be a foundational
mechanism for coordination in distributed systems of all scales
and complexities.
